options.text: Overeating and lack of exercise
Autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta cells
Excess insulin production
A viral infection of the thyroid gland

quiz_questions.explanation: Type 1 diabetes mellitus is an autoimmune disease in which the immune system attacks and destroys the insulin-producing beta cells of the pancreatic islets, resulting in absolute insulin deficiency requiring lifelong insulin therapy.

options.text: Complete absence of all pancreatic beta cells
Insulin resistance and relative insulin deficiency
Autoimmune destruction of the thyroid gland
Too much glucagon with normal insulin

quiz_questions.explanation: Type 2 diabetes is characterized by insulin resistance, where target cells fail to respond properly to insulin, combined with a relative insulin deficiency as beta cells cannot produce enough insulin to overcome the resistance.

quiz_questions.question_text: Graves' disease is a form of:

options.text: Hypothyroidism from iodine deficiency
Hyperthyroidism caused by autoimmune stimulation of the thyroid
Diabetes caused by excess insulin
Adrenal insufficiency

quiz_questions.explanation: Graves' disease is an autoimmune condition in which antibodies (thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulins) mimic TSH, binding to TSH receptors and continuously stimulating the thyroid to overproduce T3 and T4, causing hyperthyroidism.

options.text: Hyperthyroidism with weight loss
Hypothyroidism through gradual autoimmune thyroid destruction
Diabetes from beta cell destruction
Excess cortisol production

quiz_questions.explanation: Hashimoto's thyroiditis is the most common cause of hypothyroidism in iodine-sufficient areas, involving autoimmune lymphocytic infiltration that gradually destroys thyroid tissue, leading to progressive decline in thyroid hormone production.

options.text: Weight loss, low blood sugar, and hypotension
Moon face, buffalo hump, central obesity, easy bruising, and hyperglycemia
Exophthalmos, weight loss, and heat intolerance
Excessive linear growth in children

quiz_questions.explanation: Cushing's syndrome results from chronic cortisol excess and presents with characteristic features including redistribution of fat (moon face, buffalo hump, central obesity), thin skin with easy bruising, hyperglycemia, hypertension, and muscle wasting.

options.text: Excess cortisol from an adrenal tumor
Adrenal insufficiency with low cortisol and aldosterone
Excess aldosterone causing high blood pressure
A thyroid disorder causing weight gain

quiz_questions.explanation: Addison's disease (primary adrenal insufficiency) results from destruction of the adrenal cortex, leading to deficiencies of cortisol, aldosterone, and adrenal androgens, causing hypotension, hyperkalemia, hyperpigmentation, and fatigue.

quiz_questions.question_text: Gigantism occurs when excess growth hormone is produced:

options.text: After the growth plates have closed in adulthood
Before the growth plates close during childhood
At any age regardless of growth plate status
Only in the elderly population

quiz_questions.explanation: Gigantism occurs when excess GH is present during childhood before the epiphyseal plates fuse, allowing excessive linear bone growth that results in abnormally tall stature, as opposed to acromegaly which occurs after growth plate closure.

quiz_questions.question_text: Acromegaly occurs when excess growth hormone is produced:

options.text: During fetal development only
After growth plates close in adulthood, enlarging extremities and facial features
During infancy exclusively
It never occurs in adults

quiz_questions.explanation: Acromegaly results from GH excess in adults after epiphyseal plate closure; since bones can no longer grow longer, they grow wider, leading to enlarged hands, feet, jaw, and facial features, along with soft tissue enlargement.

options.text: High blood sugar from insulin deficiency
ADH deficiency or resistance leading to excessive dilute urine and extreme thirst
Excess insulin causing hypoglycemia
Excess aldosterone causing hypertension

quiz_questions.explanation: Diabetes insipidus is caused by insufficient ADH production (central type) or kidney resistance to ADH (nephrogenic type), resulting in the inability to concentrate urine, leading to excessive dilute urine output (polyuria) and compensatory extreme thirst (polydipsia).

quiz_questions.question_text: Pheochromocytoma is a tumor of the:

options.text: Thyroid gland
Adrenal medulla that produces excess catecholamines causing episodic hypertension
Anterior pituitary gland
Pancreatic islets

quiz_questions.explanation: A pheochromocytoma is a rare catecholamine-secreting tumor of the adrenal medulla chromaffin cells that causes episodic release of epinephrine and norepinephrine, producing paroxysmal hypertension, palpitations, headache, and sweating.

options.text: An autoimmune disorder of the adrenal glands
An autoimmune form of hyperthyroidism in which antibodies mimic TSH and overstimulate the thyroid gland
A genetic condition causing growth hormone deficiency
A tumor of the pituitary gland

quiz_questions.explanation: Graves' disease is the most common cause of hyperthyroidism, in which thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulins (autoantibodies) bind TSH receptors on thyroid cells and continuously stimulate hormone production, leading to symptoms including weight loss, tachycardia, heat intolerance, and often exophthalmos.

options.text: A condition of sunken eyes due to hypothyroidism
Abnormal protrusion (bulging) of the eyes, commonly associated with Graves' disease due to autoimmune inflammation of orbital tissues
Blurred vision caused by diabetes
A tumor growing behind the eye

quiz_questions.explanation: Exophthalmos is the forward displacement of the eyeballs caused by autoimmune inflammation and swelling of the orbital fat and extraocular muscles behind the eyes, and is a hallmark sign of Graves' disease that can persist even after thyroid function is corrected.

options.text: A cancerous growth in the pituitary gland
An enlargement of the thyroid gland that can occur in both hyperthyroidism and hypothyroidism
A normal variation in neck anatomy
Swelling of the parathyroid glands

quiz_questions.explanation: A goiter is a visible enlargement of the thyroid gland that can result from various causes including iodine deficiency (endemic goiter), Graves' disease, Hashimoto's thyroiditis, or thyroid nodules, and may occur with either overactive or underactive thyroid function.

options.text: A condition of excess cortisol from the adrenal cortex
Primary hyperaldosteronism, in which excess aldosterone causes hypertension and low potassium (hypokalemia)
A condition of excess growth hormone in adulthood
A form of diabetes caused by insulin resistance

quiz_questions.explanation: Conn's syndrome (primary hyperaldosteronism) is caused by an aldosterone-producing adrenal adenoma, leading to excess aldosterone that causes excessive sodium retention (raising blood pressure) and potassium excretion (causing hypokalemia, muscle weakness, and cardiac risk).

options.text: A condition of insufficient ADH causing excessive urination
Syndrome of inappropriate ADH secretion, in which excess ADH causes water retention and dilutional hyponatremia
A syndrome of insulin resistance in adipose tissue
An autoimmune disease attacking the posterior pituitary

quiz_questions.explanation: SIADH occurs when ADH is secreted in excess regardless of blood osmolarity, causing the kidneys to retain too much water, which dilutes blood sodium to dangerously low levels (hyponatremia) and can lead to confusion, seizures, and cerebral edema.

options.text: Excess thyroid hormone in adults causing weight loss
A condition resulting from severe thyroid deficiency during fetal or infant development, causing intellectual disability and growth failure
A form of pituitary dwarfism in teenagers
An adrenal tumor found in newborns

quiz_questions.explanation: Cretinism (congenital hypothyroidism) results from severe thyroid hormone deficiency during critical periods of fetal and infant brain development, causing irreversible intellectual disability, growth retardation, and developmental delay if not detected and treated early with thyroid hormone replacement.

options.text: A condition caused solely by ovarian cysts with no hormonal involvement
A complex endocrine disorder involving hormonal imbalance with excess androgens, irregular menstrual periods, and often insulin resistance
A form of ovarian cancer requiring surgical removal
An autoimmune condition that destroys the ovaries

quiz_questions.explanation: PCOS is one of the most common endocrine disorders in women of reproductive age, characterized by excess androgen production, irregular or absent ovulation, and frequently associated with insulin resistance, affecting fertility, metabolism, and overall health.

options.text: A form of diabetes caused by insulin deficiency
A condition caused by ADH deficiency or resistance, characterized by production of large volumes of dilute urine and intense thirst, unrelated to blood sugar
A condition caused by excess insulin leading to low blood sugar
A type of kidney cancer

quiz_questions.explanation: Despite sharing the name 'diabetes' (meaning excessive urination), diabetes insipidus is entirely different from diabetes mellitus; it involves either insufficient ADH production (central) or kidney resistance to ADH (nephrogenic), causing inability to concentrate urine and producing large volumes of dilute urine.

options.text: A condition of abnormally low thyroid hormone levels
A condition of excessive thyroid hormone production causing increased metabolism, weight loss, rapid heart rate, and heat intolerance
A normal state of thyroid function
An enlarged thyroid gland with normal function

quiz_questions.explanation: Hyperthyroidism (hyper = excessive) is a condition where the thyroid gland produces too much T3 and T4, leading to a hypermetabolic state with symptoms including unintentional weight loss, tachycardia, tremor, anxiety, heat intolerance, and increased sweating.

options.text: Growth hormone deficiency in children causing short stature
A condition caused by excess growth hormone in adulthood, resulting in enlargement of the hands, feet, jaw, and facial features after growth plates have closed
A calcium disorder causing bone loss
An autoimmune condition attacking the pituitary gland

quiz_questions.explanation: Acromegaly (from Greek 'acro' = extremity, 'megaly' = enlargement) is caused by a GH-secreting pituitary adenoma in adults; since long bones cannot grow after epiphyseal closure, excess GH causes widening of bones, enlargement of hands, feet, and jaw, soft tissue thickening, and organ enlargement.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 12-year-old child is diagnosed with type 1 diabetes after presenting to the emergency room with extreme thirst, frequent urination, and weight loss despite increased appetite. Blood tests show very high glucose and ketones. Why did this child develop ketones?

options.text: Ketones are a normal byproduct of glucose metabolism when insulin is present
Without insulin, cells cannot use glucose for energy, so the body breaks down fat as an alternative fuel, producing ketone bodies as a byproduct
The child ate too many fatty foods, which directly produce ketones
Ketones come from excess glucagon production by the thyroid gland

quiz_questions.explanation: In type 1 diabetes, the complete lack of insulin prevents cells from taking up glucose, forcing the body to rely on fat breakdown (lipolysis) for energy; the liver converts fatty acids into ketone bodies, which accumulate in the blood causing diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA), a life-threatening emergency.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 35-year-old woman comes to her doctor complaining of heart palpitations, unintentional weight loss of 15 pounds, excessive sweating, and anxiety. On examination, the doctor notices her eyes appear to bulge outward. Which endocrine disorder is most likely, and what is causing the eye changes?

options.text: Hypothyroidism causing fluid retention behind the eyes
Graves' disease (autoimmune hyperthyroidism), with exophthalmos caused by autoimmune inflammation of the orbital tissues behind the eyes
Cushing's syndrome causing fat deposits behind the eyes
Diabetes mellitus causing retinal swelling

quiz_questions.explanation: This presentation is classic Graves' disease: the combination of hyperthyroid symptoms (weight loss, palpitations, sweating, anxiety) with exophthalmos (bulging eyes) is pathognomonic; the eye changes result from autoimmune inflammation and edema of the orbital fat and extraocular muscles.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient who has been on high-dose prednisone for several months develops a round face, fat deposits on the upper back, purple stretch marks on the abdomen, and high blood sugar. What disorder does this clinical picture resemble, and what is the cause?

options.text: Addison's disease from too little cortisol
Iatrogenic (drug-induced) Cushing's syndrome from chronic excess exogenous cortisol
Graves' disease from excess thyroid hormone
Type 1 diabetes from autoimmune beta cell destruction

quiz_questions.explanation: Long-term use of exogenous corticosteroids like prednisone produces iatrogenic Cushing's syndrome, with all the hallmark features of chronic cortisol excess: moon face, buffalo hump, central obesity, striae (stretch marks from thinned skin), and steroid-induced hyperglycemia.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient with Addison's disease is under significant stress from a severe illness. Despite usually managing well with daily oral cortisol replacement, the patient becomes dangerously hypotensive, confused, and weak. What is happening?

options.text: The patient has developed hyperthyroidism
The patient is experiencing an adrenal crisis because the usual replacement dose is insufficient for the increased cortisol demand during severe stress
The patient's aldosterone levels are too high
The patient has developed type 2 diabetes from the cortisol replacement

quiz_questions.explanation: In Addison's disease, the adrenal glands cannot mount a stress response; during illness or surgery, cortisol demand increases dramatically, and the usual replacement dose becomes insufficient, potentially causing an adrenal crisis with life-threatening hypotension, hyponatremia, and hyperkalemia requiring emergency IV hydrocortisone.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 55-year-old man visits his doctor after noticing his shoes and rings no longer fit. His jaw has become more prominent and his hands have grown noticeably larger over the past several years. Which endocrine disorder is this, and what is the most likely cause?

options.text: Gigantism from excess GH during childhood
Acromegaly, most likely caused by a GH-secreting pituitary adenoma producing excess growth hormone after growth plates have fused
Pituitary dwarfism from GH deficiency
Osteoporosis causing bone remodeling

quiz_questions.explanation: Gradual enlargement of the hands, feet, and jaw in an adult is the hallmark of acromegaly, which is most commonly caused by a GH-secreting pituitary adenoma; because epiphyseal plates are closed, excess GH causes periosteal bone growth and soft tissue enlargement rather than increased height.

quiz_questions.question_text: A mother brings her infant to the pediatrician because the baby seems lethargic, has a swollen tongue, and appears to be developing more slowly than other infants of the same age. Newborn screening reveals very low thyroid hormone levels. What condition does the baby most likely have, and why is early treatment critical?

options.text: Graves' disease, which will resolve on its own
Congenital hypothyroidism (cretinism), which requires immediate thyroid hormone replacement to prevent irreversible intellectual disability and growth failure
Type 1 diabetes requiring insulin treatment
Normal developmental variation that needs no intervention

quiz_questions.explanation: Congenital hypothyroidism (cretinism) presents with lethargy, macroglossia (enlarged tongue), and developmental delay; thyroid hormones are critical for brain development, so early detection through newborn screening and immediate levothyroxine replacement are essential to prevent permanent intellectual disability.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient is found to have hypertension that does not respond to typical blood pressure medications. Blood work reveals very high aldosterone levels and very low potassium. The physician suspects an adrenal tumor. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Pheochromocytoma from excess catecholamines
Conn's syndrome (primary hyperaldosteronism), in which an adrenal adenoma produces excess aldosterone causing sodium retention, hypertension, and potassium wasting
Cushing's syndrome from excess cortisol
Addison's disease from adrenal insufficiency

quiz_questions.explanation: The combination of resistant hypertension, elevated aldosterone, and hypokalemia is classic for Conn's syndrome; the aldosterone-producing adenoma causes excessive sodium and water retention (raising blood pressure) and inappropriate potassium excretion (causing dangerous hypokalemia).

quiz_questions.question_text: A college student is brought to the emergency room after episodes of sudden severe headaches, rapid heart rate, profuse sweating, and dangerously high blood pressure that comes and goes. An abdominal CT scan reveals a mass in the adrenal gland. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Conn's syndrome from excess aldosterone
Pheochromocytoma, a catecholamine-secreting adrenal medulla tumor causing paroxysmal hypertension, headaches, palpitations, and diaphoresis
Addison's disease from adrenal cortex failure
Type 2 diabetes from insulin resistance

quiz_questions.explanation: The classic triad of episodic headaches, palpitations/tachycardia, and diaphoresis (sweating) with paroxysmal hypertension strongly suggests pheochromocytoma, a rare tumor of the adrenal medulla that episodically releases massive amounts of catecholamines (epinephrine and norepinephrine).

quiz_questions.question_text: A 25-year-old woman visits her gynecologist for irregular periods, difficulty losing weight, and excess facial hair growth. Lab work shows elevated testosterone and insulin levels. Ultrasound reveals multiple small follicles on both ovaries. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Cushing's syndrome from cortisol excess
Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), characterized by excess androgens, irregular cycles, and insulin resistance
Graves' disease causing hypermetabolism
Addison's disease causing adrenal insufficiency

quiz_questions.explanation: PCOS is diagnosed based on at least two of three criteria: irregular periods (oligo/anovulation), excess androgens (causing hirsutism and acne), and polycystic ovaries on ultrasound; the associated insulin resistance exacerbates androgen production and contributes to weight management difficulties.

quiz_questions.question_text: A post-surgical patient who had a brain tumor removed near the pituitary gland begins producing 8-10 liters of very dilute urine per day and is extremely thirsty. Blood sugar levels are normal. What endocrine disorder has developed, and what caused it?

options.text: Type 1 diabetes mellitus from surgical stress on the pancreas
Central diabetes insipidus from surgical damage to the hypothalamus or posterior pituitary, disrupting ADH production or release
SIADH from excess ADH production after surgery
Nephrogenic diabetes insipidus from kidney damage during surgery

quiz_questions.explanation: Surgery near the pituitary can damage the hypothalamic-neurohypophyseal tract that produces and transports ADH; without ADH, the kidneys cannot concentrate urine, leading to massive volumes of dilute urine (polyuria) and compensatory thirst (polydipsia), the hallmarks of central diabetes insipidus.
